segments.inferno-27-g29: Io era is the imperfect of essere ('I was'), giving a continuing past state. In giuso (archaic spelling of giù) is an adverbial phrase 'down below'; ancora modifies the state ('still'). Attento e chino are predicate adjectives describing io; chino means 'bowed' here.
segments.inferno-27-g30: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate 'when'. Il mio duca is the subject ('my guide' — Virgil as guide). Mi tentò is passato remoto of tentare; here tentò means 'touched' (physically tested/tapped) rather than 'attempted'. Di costa is a fixed idiom 'on the side' (costa = side, of the body).
segments.inferno-27-g31: Dicendo is the gerund of dire ('saying') and introduces the direct speech that follows; it links the physical touch to the words spoken.
segments.inferno-27-g32: Parla is an imperative (or hortative) telling someone to speak; placing the verb before tu gives slight emphasis to the command 'You, speak.' Tu is the stressed second-person pronoun.
segments.inferno-27-g33: Questi is an archaic form of questo meaning 'this one' (used here of the person before them); è is the present indicative of essere; latino is a predicate noun meaning 'Latin' — i.e. a Latin-speaker/Belonging to Latium or speaking a Latinate tongue in Dante's usage.
segments.inferno-27-g34: E io simply links to the preceding speech ('And I'), with io as the subject; the comma marks the beginning of a relative clause about the speaker.
segments.inferno-27-g35: Ch’ (che) introduces a relative clause modifying io: 'who had already the answer ready.' Avea is the imperfect of avere ('had'); già = 'already'; pronta la risposta = 'the answer ready' (pronta is an adjective agreeing with risposta).
segments.inferno-27-g36: Sanza indugio is an archaic expression meaning 'without delay' (sanza = senza). A parlare incominciai places the infinitive 'to speak' before the verb incominciai (passato remoto 'I began'), a common poetic word order: 'I began to speak.'
segments.inferno-27-g37: O anima is a vocative address ('O soul'); che se’ is an archaic relative + verb where se’ = sei ('you are'); là giù nascosta is an adverbial phrase 'hidden down there' (là giù = down there; nascosta = hidden).
segments.inferno-27-g38: Romagna tua ('your Romagna') places the possessive after the place name for poetic effect; non è is the present negative 'is not' (Romagna tua non è = 'Romagna is not yours').
segments.inferno-27-g39: E non fu mai continues the negation in the past: fu is passato remoto of essere ('was'), modified by mai ('ever/never'), so 'and it never was.' This gives a historical absolute negation.
segments.inferno-27-g40: Sanza guerra = 'without war'; ne’ cuor is an archaic contraction (ne’ = nei) meaning 'in the hearts'; de’ suoi tiranni = 'of its tyrants.' The whole phrase means 'without war in the hearts of its tyrants.'
segments.inferno-27-g41: Ma introduces a contrast ('but'); ’n is a poetic contraction of in (here 'in palese' = 'in the open/publicly'); palese = 'public, manifest'; nessuna = 'no one' or 'none'; or = 'now' (presently); vi = 'there'; lasciai is passato remoto 'I left' (here 'I left none there publicly').
segments.inferno-27-g42: Main clause with a poetic/inverted perfect: 'Ravenna sta' (sta = is situated/remains) is followed by a comparative clause introduced by 'come'. The sequence 'stata è' is an archaic ordering of the participle + auxiliary ('stata' = been, 'è' = is/has been) meaning 'has been'; 'molt’ anni' (molt’ = contracted form of molti) is an adverbial time phrase 'for many years'. Overall: 'Ravenna stands/ remains as it has stood for many years.'
segments.inferno-27-g43: Independent clause: 'l’aguglia da Polenta' = 'the eagle (lord) of the Polenta [family]'. 'da' here marks origin/association ('of/by the Polenta'). The clitic sequence 'la si cova' places the direct object pronoun 'la' (referring back to Ravenna) before the reflexive/impersonal clitic 'si' and then the verb 'cova' (present indicative of covare = brood). This construction literally renders 'the Polenta eagle broods it (there)'.
segments.inferno-27-g44: 'sì che' is a conjunction meaning 'so that' or 'with the result that'. 'Cervia ricuopre' is present indicative ('ricuoprire' = cover/cover over). 'co’' is the contracted form of 'con i' ('with the'), here before the plural possessive 'suoi' (its). 'vanni' is an archaic/plural noun placed after the possessive; its precise local sense is dialectal, hence not further glossed here. The clause links as result: because the Polenta eagle broods over it, 'Cervia covers it with its vanni.'
segments.inferno-27-g45: 'La terra' is the subject; 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying it. 'fé' is an archaic preterite/perfect of fare ('did/made') used by Dante to mean 'caused or underwent', and it governs two coordinated complements: 'già la lunga prova' ('already the long trial') and 'di Franceschi sanguinoso mucchio' (a bloody throng composed of Franceschi). The adjective 'sanguinoso' follows the noun it qualifies, as is common in poetic diction.
segments.inferno-27-g46: 'sotto' is the preposition 'under', followed by the noun phrase 'le branche verdi' ('the green branches'). The verb phrase 'si ritrova' is reflexive/pronominal ('ritrovarsi') and here means 'is found' or 'lies' — the reflexive 'si' makes the verb intransitive and indicates that the subject (La terra) 'finds itself' in that place. The subject of this verb is the previously introduced 'La terra', so the clause places the locative phrase after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-27-g115: Francesco is the subject (proper name). venne is the passato remoto (third person singular) of venire meaning 'came' — typical narrative past in Italian. poi is an adverb 'then' placed after the verb for rhythm; the clause reads 'Francesco then came.'
segments.inferno-27-g116: com’ is the contracted form of come = 'as' (here introducing a circumstantial clause). io is the subject pronoun 'I' used for emphasis. fu is passato remoto of essere ('was') and morto is a past participle used adjectivally ('dead'), so com' io fu' morto = 'as I was dead/as I lay dead.'
segments.inferno-27-g117: per is a preposition here meaning 'for' or 'on behalf of' (per me = 'for me' or 'on my account'). me is the object pronoun 'me/myself' completing the prepositional phrase.
segments.inferno-27-g118: ma is the coordinating conjunction 'but'. un d’i (= uno dei) means 'one of the' and introduces the subject 'one of the black cherubim' (d’ + i = 'of the'). neri is an adjective 'black' modifying cherubini (plural); the cluster is the subject of the following verb. li here is an enclitic pronoun (rendered in the lemma list as 'the') functioning as the indirect object 'to them' or 'to those (others)'; disse is passato remoto of dire 'said.'
segments.inferno-27-g119: Non is the simple negative 'not'. portar is the infinitive 'to carry/bring' — here an imperative-like prohibition inside reported speech where the full form would be 'non portarli' ('do not carry him'); the bare infinitive appears for poetic brevity.
segments.inferno-27-g120: non mi far torto is a direct address within the quotation: non = 'do not'; mi = indirect object 'to me' (i.e. 'do me'); far is the bare infinitive of fare 'to do/make' used after non as an imperative-like prohibition; torto is a noun/adjective 'wrong' — together 'do me no wrong' or 'do not wrong me.'
segments.inferno-27-g121: Venir is an infinitive 'to come' used impersonally here (with se ne dee) to express obligation: 'it must be that he come' or more naturally 'he should be brought.' se ne dee is an archaic/poetic construction: dee is the third person singular present subjunctive/indicative-archaic form of dovere 'must/ought', ne is the pronoun 'of it/him/them' and se is reflexive/impersonal marker; giù 'down' and tra ’ miei meschini 'among my wretched ones' give the destination. Overall: 'He ought to come down among my wretches.'
segments.inferno-27-g122: dal quale = 'from which' (dal = da + il), in qua means 'from this point/now on' or 'henceforth', stato 'state' and li sono a' crini literally 'they are to the hairs' — an idiomatic way of saying 'they are in this condition down to the very hairs' (crini = 'hairs, mane'). The clause marks the lasting effect of the counsel.
segments.inferno-27-g123: ch’ (che) introduces a content clause 'that': assolver non si può = 'one cannot absolve' — non ... può is negative with può (present indicative of potere). chi non si pente = 'who does not repent' is the relative/indefinite subject 'whoever does not repent.' Note the reflexive si used with both verbs: 'assolversi' (to be absolved) and 'pentirsi' (to repent).
segments.inferno-27-g124: né introduces the coordinate negative 'nor'. pentere e volere insieme puossi: pentere (pentirsi) = 'to repent', volere = 'to will/want'; insieme = 'together/at the same time'. puossi is an archaic/poetic form (subjunctive/impersonal) of potere 'can' used impersonally here — 'nor can repenting and willing be together.' per la contradizion che nol consente = 'because of the contradiction that does not allow it' (nol = non + lo = 'it not', consente = 'allows').
segments.inferno-27-g125: Oh is an interjection of lament. me is the object pronoun 'me' (accusative/emphatic) and dolente is the present participle/adjectival form 'sorrowful/unhappy', postposed for poetic exclamation: 'Oh, sorrowful me!' or 'Oh, unhappy me!'.
segments.inferno-27-g126: come introduces a comparative/temporal clause 'as/how' (here 'how I started'). mi riscossi is passato remoto first person of riscossere 'to rouse/jerk oneself' = 'I started/was roused'. quando mi prese = 'when he seized me' (prese = passato remoto of prendere). dicendomi is a gerund 'saying to me', linking to the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-27-g127: Quotation: Forse = 'perhaps'. tu non pensavi = imperfect 'you did not think' (pensavi, imperfect of pensare, expresses a past expectation). ch’io löico fossi = ch’io + subjunctive fossi (imperfect subjunctive of essere) introduces the content of that thought: 'that I were/was ...' löico (logico) is an adjective/noun 'logical' or 'logician' (here 'a logician/dialectician'); the punctuation and exclamation mark show a mocking/direct tone.
segments.inferno-27-g47: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. ’l is the elided form of the article il (poetic apostrophe). Mastin (mastiff) is a noun in front position modified by the adjectives vecchio and nuovo; the two adjectives are coordinated (’l mastin vecchio e ’l nuovo) and da Verrucchio marks origin/place (da + proper name). The apostrophes reflect Dante's poetics/elisions.
segments.inferno-27-g48: che introduces a relative clause ('who/which') referring back to the mastiffs. fecer is the archaic passato remoto (fecero → fecer) 3rd person plural of fare, 'they made/caused'. di Montagna is a genitive/locative marking the domain affected, and il mal governo is the direct object 'the bad rule/government' placed after the locative phrase for emphasis.
segments.inferno-27-g49: là dove = 'there where'. soglion is the contracted/archaic present (sogliono) meaning 'are accustomed/usually'. fan is the contracted present (fanno) used here with an omitted infinitive construction (soglion fan ... = they are wont to do ...). d’i is di + i (of the); denti succhio literally 'a sucking of the teeth' (denti governs succhio). The phrase describes a habitual action tied to the mastiffs.
segments.inferno-27-g50: Word order places the object (Le città di Lamone e di Santerno) fronted for emphasis; conduce is the present indicative 3rd singular 'leads' with il lïoncel as its grammatical subject (il lïoncel leads the towns). dal is the contraction of da + il ('from the'), and nido bianco is a noun + adjective phrase 'white nest'. lïoncel is a diminutive 'little lion'.
segments.inferno-27-g51: che begins a relative clause modifying 'nido bianco' (or the position just described). muta is present indicative 3rd singular 'changes'. parte here means 'place/position' (it changes its place). da la state al verno is an archaic seasonal expression 'from the summer to the winter' (da + article before 'state' = 'estate' in Dante's orthography; al = a + il).
segments.inferno-27-g52: E is 'and'. quella is a demonstrative 'that one' (likely a town). cu’ is the archaic relative equivalent of che ('which/that'). il Savio is the Savio river (proper name); bagna is present indicative 3rd singular 'wets/bathes' and il fianco is the object 'the flank' (the river laps against its flank).
segments.inferno-27-g53: così com’ = 'just as'. ella is the pronoun 'it/she' referring back to the town. sie’ is Dante's archaic form (sie / sia) meaning 'is' (3rd singular); tra 'l piano e 'l monte = 'between the plain and the hill' (tra + two coordinated locatives).
segments.inferno-27-g54: tra tirannia si vive is an impersonal construction: si vive = 'one lives' or 'people live' amid tirannia ('tyranny'). e introduces a coordinate phrase stato franco: stato is a noun 'state' and franco is the adjective 'free' (a free state). The clause thus contrasts a lived experience of tyranny with the political condition of being a 'free state'.
segments.inferno-27-g55: Ora = 'now'. chi se’ is an interrogative phrase 'who are you?'; se’ is the archaic second-person singular form (sei → se’) meaning 'you are'. The phrase is a direct question addressed to the listener.
segments.inferno-27-g56: ti priego = 'I beg/ask you' (first person present). che introduces the content clause; ne = 'of it/about it' (partitive pronoun) and conte is an imperative or present subjunctive form of contare used here as 'recount/tell' (conte ne = 'tell us of it').
segments.inferno-27-g57: non esser duro più = a negative infinitive phrase 'do not be more harsh'; ch’ = che 'than'; altri sia stato = 'others have been' with sia (present subjunctive used in a comparative perfect sense) + stato (past participle 'been') producing the idea 'than others have been'. The sequence expresses a polite admonition not to exceed prior harshness.
segments.inferno-27-g58: se here is the conditional 'if' (the provided senses list did not include 'if', so marked -1). ’l is the elided article il; nome tuo = 'your name' used idiomatically as 'your reputation'. nel mondo = 'in the world'. tegna is a present subjunctive form (from a verb meaning 'to hold/keep') used here in the sense 'keeps/holds', and fronte = 'front/face' (the idiom tegna fronte = 'holds up its front' = 'keeps its reputation/head held high').
segments.inferno-27-g1: Già (adverb) is fronted for emphasis: 'already'. Era is the 3rd‑person singular imperfect of essere and gives the past state ('was'). Dritta in sù is the predicate describing la fiamma; Dante uses inverted/poetic word order (verb + adjective + noun) instead of the neutral 'la fiamma era dritta in sù'. Queta coordinates with dritta ('quiet'). Per non dir più is a parenthetical idiom 'not to say more / lest I say more' introduced by per + infinitive.
segments.inferno-27-g2: E già 'and already' continues the narration. The phrase da noi sen gia contains the pronominal/poetic cluster se(n) (se n' = 'se ne') meaning 'it away from us'—Dante omits an explicit verb here but the sense is 'it had already gone away from us'. Con la licenza del dolce poeta is an ablative/agent phrase: 'with the permission of the sweet poet'.
segments.inferno-27-g3: Quand’ is the elided form of quando ('when'); un’altra is the feminine contraction of uno + altra meaning 'another (one)'. The comma sets up a subordinate temporal clause.
segments.inferno-27-g4: Che is a relative pronoun 'who/that'. Dietro a lei is a prepositional phrase 'behind her/it'. Venìa is the imperfect of venire (3rd singular) 'was coming', giving ongoing action in the past and agreeing with the relative.
segments.inferno-27-g5: Ne is the pronominal particle referring back to the preceding thing ('of it/from it') and combines with the causative fece + infinitive volger: fece volger = 'made (us) turn'. Li occhi is the direct object 'the eyes'; a la sua cima 'to its summit'. Per introduces the cause/means 'by/through a confused sound'. Che fuor n’uscia is a relative clause: fuor = fuori 'out', n’ uscia is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'issued from it' (ne + usciva).
segments.inferno-27-g6: Come introduces a simile: 'as'. ’l is the elided article (il) before bue. Cicilian (= Sicilian) qualifies bue. Che introduces the relative clause; mugghiò is passato remoto 'bellowed' and prima 'before/earlier'. Col is contraction of con + il ('with the'); pianto di colui = 'the weeping/lament of that one'.
segments.inferno-27-g7: E ciò fu dritto: e 'and', ciò 'that/this', fu passato remoto of essere 'was', and dritto here has the idiomatic meaning 'right' or 'just so' — 'and rightly so'.
segments.inferno-27-g8: Che is a relative pronoun. L’avea is the combination of clitic object l' (it) + avea (imperfect of avere) and here functions like a pluperfect/preterite auxiliary: 'he had tempered it'. Temperato is the past participle 'tempered/tuned'; con sua lima 'with his file' (lima = metal file used to tune or smooth).
segments.inferno-27-g9: Mugghiava is the imperfect 'was bellowing' (ongoing/past habitual). Con la voce de l’afflitto is an ablative/imitative phrase 'with the voice/tone of the afflicted' — the ox sounded like someone in grief.
segments.inferno-27-g10: Sì che is a conjunction meaning 'so that' or 'with the result that', introducing the consequence clause that follows.
segments.inferno-27-g11: Con tutto che is a concessive expression 'although/even though' and is followed by fosse, the imperfect subjunctive of essere ('it was'). Di rame 'of copper' indicates material; the clause con tutto che fosse di rame means 'even though it was made of copper'.
segments.inferno-27-g12: Pur = nevertheless/still. El is the dialectal/poetic form of 'egli/esso' (he/it). Pareva is imperfect 'seemed'. Dal dolor trafitto is a passive‑like construction: 'pierced by grief' (trafitto = past participle).
segments.inferno-27-g13: Così = 'thus/so', linking the previous simile to the next statement.
segments.inferno-27-g14: Per non aver = literally 'for not having' and here indicates cause/reason ('because there was no…'). Via 'way/passage' and né forame 'nor opening' give the meaning 'no passage nor opening'; dal principio = 'from the beginning'; nel foco = 'in the fire'.
segments.inferno-27-g15: In suo linguaggio = 'into/into its language/speech' (in + possessive). Si convertïan is the imperfect (they converted/turned) with a reflexive si; the diaeresis on ï marks the vowel separation. Le parole grame = 'the bitter/harsh words' (subject of the verb), so the clause means 'the bitter words were turned into its language'.
segments.inferno-27-g16: Ma poscia ch’ = 'but after that' (poscia = afterwards). Ebber colto is passato remoto 3rd plural of avere + past participle colto = 'they had taken/attained' (a perfect/pluperfect sense in temporal clause). Lor vïaggio = 'their passage/journey'. Su per la punta = 'up along/to the point/summit'.
segments.inferno-27-g17: Dandole is a gerund or participial form 'giving it' where the clitic -le refers to 'punta' (giving the point a jerk). Quel guizzo 'that sudden twitch/jerk'. Che dato avea la lingua in lor passaggio is a relative clause: dato avea (avea = pluperfect) = 'which the tongue had given' (la lingua = subject), 'in lor passaggio' 'in their passage'.
segments.inferno-27-g18: Udimmo is passato remoto 'we heard'; dire is the infinitive 'say'. The construction udimmo dire = 'we heard [them] say'.
segments.inferno-27-g19: O tu a cu’ io drizzo la voce = 'O you to whom I direct my voice': a cu' is an elided form of a cui ('to whom'), io drizzo is present indicative 'I turn/direct'. E che parlavi mo lombardo = 'and you who were speaking just now in Lombard/dialect' (parlavi = imperfect 'you were speaking', mo = 'now/just now').
segments.inferno-27-g20: Dicendo introduces the quoted words ('saying'). The quoted phrase includes dialectal/colloquial items (Istra ten va) that are preserved unmodified; va is imperative/present 'go', ten is a clitic/dialect form. The gerund dicendo links the reported speech to the previous verb (we heard say...).
segments.inferno-27-g21: Più non t’adizzo is the continuation of the quoted speech: più non = 'no longer', t’ adizzo is 'I urge/incite you' (adizzo = 1st singular present). The negative + verb expresses 'I no longer urge you'.
segments.inferno-27-g22: Perch’ io sia giunto forse alquanto tardo = 'because I may have arrived perhaps somewhat late'. Perch' = 'because'; sia giunto uses the present subjunctive perfect (subjunctive of essere + past participle) to express possibility/uncertainty ('may have arrived'). Forse/alquanto/tardo qualify the degree ('perhaps somewhat late').
segments.inferno-27-g23: Non t’incresca is a negative imperative/subjunctive formula meaning 'do not be unwilling / let it not displease you' (incresca is present subjunctive of increscare). Restare a parlar meco = 'to remain to speak with me' — an infinitival complement introduced by restare + a + infinitive.
segments.inferno-27-g24: Vedi is the imperative (or present 2nd person) 'see / note', followed by a that‑clause: che non incresce a me = 'that it does not trouble me' (incresce present indicative 3rd singular with indirect object a me).
segments.inferno-27-g25: E ardo! is a dramatic exclamation: e 'and', ardo 1st person singular present indicative of ardere 'I burn' (here figurative, 'I burn with desire/passion' or 'I am eager').
segments.inferno-27-g26: Se tu pur mo = 'if you even now' (pur mo = just now / even now). In questo mondo cieco caduto se’ is inverted/poetic order: caduto se’ = sei caduto 'you have fallen' (past participle + clitic form of essere), meaning 'have fallen into this blind world'. Di quella dolce terra latina = 'from that sweet Latin land'. Ond’ io mia colpa tutta reco = 'whence/from which I bring all my guilt' — reco is an archaic present form 'I bring/carry'.
segments.inferno-27-g27: Dimmi = imperative 'tell me'. Se introduces an indirect question 'whether'. Romagnuoli = inhabitants of Romagna; han is the contracted present of avere (hanno) 'have'. Pace o guerra = 'peace or war' — asking about the state of affairs.
segments.inferno-27-g28: Ch’io fui = 'that I was' (fui = past of essere). D’i monti là intra Orbino = 'from the mountains there within Urbino' (intra = within). E ’l giogo di che Tever si diserra = 'and the ridge/yoke from which the Tiber discharges/parts' (si diserra here a poetic verb meaning the river issues or breaks away). The whole clause gives the speaker's origin.
segments.inferno-27-g99: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'. come = 'as' introduces a comparison. Costantin is the proper name 'Constantine'. chiese is passato remoto (simple past) of chiedere, 'he asked/requested'. d’ + entro is a compressed prepositional phrase ('from/within') where d' = di ('from/of') and entro = 'within/inside'. Siratti is a proper name/place in the text (here left untranslated). a guerir uses the preposition a + infinitive guerir (older form of guarire) meaning 'in order to heal' or 'to cure'. de = 'of/from' and la lebbre = 'the leprosy' (lebbre is the archaic plural form used for the disease).
segments.inferno-27-g100: così = 'thus/so', linking the comparison to the present case. mi is the indirect object clitic 'me' (to me). chiese again is passato remoto 'he asked'. questi = 'this one/this man' (demonstrative used as a pronoun). per maestro means 'as / to be (my) master/teacher' (per often = 'as' in older usage). a guerir de la sua superba febbre = 'to heal of his proud fever', where su(a) = his and superba is an adjective 'proud' qualifying febbre ('fever'); guerir is infinitive 'to heal'.
segments.inferno-27-g101: domandommi is a present participle/gerund-like form built on domandare + enclitic mi, literally 'asking me' (older orthography/formation). consiglio is the noun 'advice/counsel' functioning as the object of domandommi.
segments.inferno-27-g102: e io tacetti: e = 'and'; io is the subject 'I'; tacetti is passato remoto of tacere, here meaning 'I fell silent / I kept silent' (an older verb form rather than the senses listed). perché = 'because'; le sue parole = 'his words/speech' (le = direct object article + sue = his); parver is an archaic form meaning 'seemed/appeared' (third person plural/archaic variant of parvero/parve) and ebbre is plural/adjective 'drunk/intoxicated' (agreeing with parole).
segments.inferno-27-g103: E’ is an archaic/clipped form for 'egli' or similar subject pronoun ('he'); poi = 'then/afterwards'; ridisse is passato remoto of ridire used here as 'he said again' or 'he repeated'.
segments.inferno-27-g104: Tuo cuor = 'your heart' (tuo = possessive before a noun in poetic register). non sospetti is a jussive/optative use of the present subjunctive (sospetti) meaning 'may/must not suspect' or 'let not ... suspect' — Dante uses the subjunctive here in direct speech to express a wish or order.
segments.inferno-27-g105: finor = archaic form of finora 'until now'; t' = clitic for 'you' (to you); assolvo is present indicative 'I absolve/forgive/acquit' (first person singular). The phrase means 'up to now I absolve you.'
segments.inferno-27-g106: e tu m’insegna fare: e = 'and'; tu = 'you' (subject); m' = mi, indirect object 'me'; insegna here is used as a verb 'teach' (older/poetic present indicative 2nd person), followed by the infinitive fare 'to do/make'. sì come introduces a comparison 'just as/so as'; Penestrino is a diminutive noun ('little window') used metaphorically; in terra getti = 'throws/casts into the earth' (getti is present subj./indicative/poetic form of gettare or third-person present).
segments.inferno-27-g107: Lo ciel = 'the heaven' (Lo is the article used before a masculine noun in older/poetic register); poss’ io is an inversion/elliptical form of posso io ('I can'), with the apocopated poss' used for meter; serrare e diserrare are infinitives (or a paired verb phrase) meaning 'to lock/close and to unlock/open' — literal ability ('I can close and open heaven').
segments.inferno-27-g108: come tu sai = 'as you know'; come = 'as', tu = 'you', sai = present indicative of sapere 'you know', a straightforward parenthetical clause asserting the listener's knowledge.
segments.inferno-27-g109: però = 'therefore' or 'thus'; son is the contracted poetic form of sono ('are'); due le chiavi = 'two are the keys' (inverted word order typical of poetry); che introduces a relative clause: ’l mio antecessor = 'my predecessor' (antecessor is older spelling); non ebbe care = passato remoto of avere + care (care = 'dear/precious') meaning 'did not have (them) dear' i.e. 'did not value them' or 'did not hold them dear'.
segments.inferno-27-g110: Allor = 'then' (archaic/poetic). mi pinser is passato remoto (pinser) with the indirect object clitic mi: here pinser functions like 'pressed upon me' or 'impressed me' (an older poetic verb form); li argomenti gravi = 'the serious/weighty arguments' (li = article; argomenti = arguments/reasons; gravi = 'grave/serious'). là ’ve = 'there where' (là + ve = dove/where); ’l tacer mi fu avviso = 'the silence was a warning to me' (tacer = the infinitive 'silence/keeping silent' used as a noun; mi fu avviso = 'was a warning to me'), and ’l peggio = 'the worse/the worst' (peggio used substantively). The whole clause locates where the pressure of argument was felt, with compacted word order for meter.
segments.inferno-27-g111: e dissi = 'and I said' (e = and; dissi = passato remoto of dire, 'I said'). This introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-27-g112: Padre is direct address 'Father' (vocative) — a single-word interjection opening the speech.
segments.inferno-27-g113: da che tu mi lavi: da che is a temporal/causal connector here best rendered 'since' or 'now that'; tu mi lavi is present indicative 'you wash/cleanse me' (lavi = lavare). di quel peccato = 'of/from that sin' (di = of/from; quel = that; peccato = sin). ov’ = archaic contraction of 'ove' meaning 'wherein/into which'; io mo cader deggio = io = I; mo = now; cader = infinitive 'to fall'; deggio is a poetical present/future sense 'I must / I ought' (first-person present indicative used with a modal force). The clause means roughly 'since you cleanse me from that sin into which I must now fall.'
segments.inferno-27-g114: lunga promessa = 'a long promise' (adjective before noun for emphasis). con l’attender corto = 'with brief waiting' (con = with; l' + attender = the gerund/infinitive notion 'waiting/expectation' in older form; corto = short/brief). ti farà trïunfar = 'will make you triumph' (farà = future of fare 'will make'; trïunfar = 'to triumph' — note the dieresis indicating pronunciation), ne l’alto seggio = 'in the high/lofty seat' (ne = in; l’ alto seggio = the lofty seat/throne). Word order is compact and somewhat inverted for metrical reasons.
segments.inferno-27-g83: Fronted direct objects (Li accorgimenti e le coperte vie) precede the subject clitic and verb for poetic emphasis. "Li" is the masculine plural article; "accorgimenti" means schemes/stratagems. "Le coperte vie" literally 'the covered/hidden ways' (an adjectival noun phrase). "Io seppi" is passato remoto (seppi = I knew/found out) with the subject pronoun "io" placed before the verb for emphasis; "tutte" (feminine plural) agrees with "vie" and indicates completeness.
segments.inferno-27-g84: "e sì menai lor arte" uses "menai" (passato remoto of menare) meaning 'I led/brought' or more idiomatically 'I put into practice/used'. "sì" is an intensifier 'thus/so', and "lor" is the archaic clitic for "loro" ('their'). "arte" here means skill/cunning — 'their craft/cunning' — and the verb governs it as the thing he applied or carried out.
segments.inferno-27-g85: The clause begins with the contraction "ch'" (= che, 'that/so that'). "al fine de la terra" = 'to/at the end of the earth' (a + il = al; "fine" = end/boundary; "la terra" = the earth/the world). "il suono" is the subject placed after the locative phrase for poetic inversion. "uscie" is an archaic past form of uscire (3rd sg. passato remoto) meaning 'went out/was heard/issued forth'.
segments.inferno-27-g86: "Quando mi vidi giunto" is a reflexive/periphrastic past: "vidi" (passato remoto of vedere) + past participle "giunto" = 'I found myself arrived' / 'I realized I had reached'. The phrase "in quella parte di mia etade" is a temporal/place expression 'in that part of my age/life'. "ove ciascun dovrebbe calar le vele e raccoglier le sarte" uses "ove" = 'where', and "dovrebbe" (present conditional) expresses what one ought/should do; the nautical metaphors "calar le vele" (lower the sails) and "raccoglier le sarte" (take in/furl the rigging) indicate slowing/retiring from active life.
segments.inferno-27-g87: "ciò che pria mi piacëa" is a relative clause: "ciò che" = 'that which'. "pria" is an archaic adverb meaning 'before/previously'; "piacëa" is an imperfect form ('pleased me' / 'used to please me'), so the clause means 'that which formerly pleased me'.
segments.inferno-27-g88: "allor m’increbbe" = 'then it displeased me.' "allor" = 'then' (archaic); the clitic "m'" (to me) precedes the verb; "increbbe" is an impersonal/3rd-person form meaning 'it displeased' (compare modern 'mi dispiacque' / 'mi dispiaceva').
segments.inferno-27-g89: "e pentuto e confesso mi rendei;" has two predicative adjectives "pentuto e confesso" ('penitent and having confessed') describing the subject, followed by "mi rendei" (passato remoto of rendersi, 'I rendered myself / I made myself' → idiomatically 'I became penitent and confessed' or 'I gave myself up (in penitence)'). The reflexive construction makes the subject the experiencer of the change.
segments.inferno-27-g90: "ahi miser lasso!" is an exclamatory interjection. "ahi" = 'ah/alas'; "miser" = 'wretched/poor' (addressing himself as wretch); "lasso" = 'weary' / 'wretched' — a conventional lament in Dante.
segments.inferno-27-g91: "e giovato sarebbe" is a conditional construction: "sarebbe" (conditional of essere, 'would be') with the past participle "giovato" ('been of use/been beneficial'). Together they express 'and it would have been helpful/profitable (to me)'. The linking "e" ties this remark to the previous lament.
segments.inferno-27-g92: "Lo principe d’i novi Farisei" is a nominative apposition: "Lo principe" = 'the prince' (article + noun); "d'i novi Farisei" = 'of the new Pharisees' (di + i = d'i). The phrase names the leader of a contemporary hypocritical party (Farisees used metaphorically).
segments.inferno-27-g93: "avendo guerra presso a Laterano" uses the gerund/gerund-like "avendo" ('having' / 'waging') to give the circumstance: 'waging war near the Lateran.' "presso a" = 'near/close to' and Laterano is the place name (the Lateran).
segments.inferno-27-g94: "e non con Saracin né con Giudei" is a straightforward negative coordination: "non...né...né" = 'not...nor...'. "con" = 'with/against' (here 'against'), so 'not against Saracens nor against Jews.'
segments.inferno-27-g95: "ché ciascun suo nimico era cristiano" begins with causal "ché" = 'because/for'. "ciascun suo nimico" = 'each one of his enemies'; "era" is imperfect 'was' describing their identity ('was Christian'). This clause explains why the war was not against Saracens or Jews.
segments.inferno-27-g96: "e nessun era stato a vincer Acri né mercatante in terra di Soldano" uses a compound past (era stato = 'had been') with infinitive "vincer" meaning 'to conquer/take.' The phrase says no one had gone to conquer Acre nor had been a merchant in the Sultan's land — i.e., he lacked experience of crusade or eastern trade. "né ... né ..." again coordinates two negated predicates.
segments.inferno-27-g97: "né sommo officio né ordini sacri guardò in sé" continues the negation: 'nor did he look to (seek) the highest office nor to sacred orders in himself.' "guardò in sé" (passato remoto) literally 'he looked in himself' = 'he considered/regarded in himself' — i.e., he did not contemplate holding the supreme office or entering holy orders.
segments.inferno-27-g98: "né in me quel capestro che solea fare i suoi cinti più macri" completes the object of "guardò": 'nor did he see in me that halter/constraint which used to make his belts (i.e. his followers' belts) leaner.' "capestro" is a metaphorical 'halter/noose' (a restraint or chastening device); "solea fare" = 'was wont to make' (solea = used to), and "i suoi cinti più macri" = 'his belts more meagre/lean' (metaphor for austerity).
segments.inferno-27-g59: Poscia che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'after' (literary). '’l foco' = il foco, subject 'the fire'. 'ebbe rugghiato' uses ebbe (passato remoto of avere) as auxiliary with the past participle rugghiato (rugghiare) — a literary/perfective past: 'had roared'. 'al modo suo' = 'in its own way' (al = a + il).
segments.inferno-27-g60: L’aguta punta is a noun phrase 'the sharp point' (aguta = archaic form of acuta). Mòsse is passato remoto of muovere ('moved'); here it governs the adverbial complements 'di qua' (this way). The clause is terse/poetic word order (adjective before noun; verb after).
segments.inferno-27-g61: Di là is a short adverbial phrase 'to that side' or 'that way', balancing di qua; it completes the motion expressed by mosse.
segments.inferno-27-g62: E poi 'and then'. Diè is passato remoto of dare 'gave' (here 'gave a blast/puff'). Cotal is an archaic form of cotale 'such', and fiato = 'breath, puff, blast'. The colon introduces the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-27-g63: S’i’ credesse is a contracted conditional equivalent to Se io credesse 'If I believed' (credesse = imperfect/subjunctive used for counterfactual/hypothetical). Mia risposta fosse is a subordinate clause with fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) ('were my answer to...'). A persona che mai tornasse al mondo is a relative clause; tornasse is imperfect subjunctive because the speaker imagines the possibility (who might ever return to earthly life).
segments.inferno-27-g64: Questa fiamma is the subject 'this flame'. Staria is the conditional of stare ('would remain' / 'would stay'). Sanza is an archaic spelling of senza ('without'). Più scosse = 'further disturbances' (scosse here is plural noun derived from past participle scosso 'shakings').
segments.inferno-27-g65: Ma però che introduces an explanatory clause ('but because/forasmuch as'). Già mai is a poetic adverbial cluster meaning 'never yet' (già often strengthens the negation). 'Di questo fondo' = 'from this depth'; non tornò vivo alcun uses passato remoto tornò 'returned' and alcun after negation means 'no one/none'.
segments.inferno-27-g66: S’i’ odo il vero is a concessive/parenthetical 'if I hear the truth' (Se io odo il vero) used like 'if I am not mistaken'. Odo is present indicative 'I hear'. The clause qualifies the speaker's confidence.
segments.inferno-27-g67: Sanza = senza 'without'. Tema d’infamia = 'fear of infamy/disgrace' (tema 'fear' + di infamia). Ti is the indirect-object pronoun 'to you', and rispondo is present indicative 'I answer' used to state the speaker's present reply; the clause is the conclusion of the direct speech.
segments.inferno-27-g68: Io fui uom d’arme: fui is passato remoto (I was). 'Uom d’arme' = 'man of arms' (man-at-arms, soldier). The noun phrase follows the subject and verb in typical poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-27-g69: E poi fui cordigliero: poi 'then/afterwards'. Cordigliero is a noun meaning a cord-wearing friar (Franciscan); fui again is past 'I was'. The sequence lists successive states/roles.
segments.inferno-27-g70: Credendomi is a present gerund/participle form 'believing myself' or 'thinking (myself)' where -end- marks the gerund participle; it modifies the subject and connects to the infinitive clause that follows.
segments.inferno-27-g71: Sì cinto: sì = 'so/thus'; cinto is past participle/adjective 'girded' (from cingere) describing his state (girded with the cord). Poetic adjective-noun order is compact.
segments.inferno-27-g72: Fare ammenda: infinitive 'to make amends' (fare = 'to do/make'; ammenda = 'amends/atonement'). This expresses the friar's intended purpose.
segments.inferno-27-g73: E certo il creder mio venìa intero: 'e certo' = 'and certainly'; il creder mio = 'my belief' (creder as a noun), venìa is imperfect/conditional nuance 'would come/be' (here 'would have been'), intero = 'whole/intact'. The imperfect expresses what would have been the case.
segments.inferno-27-g74: Se non fosse il gran prete: se non fosse = 'if it were not' (imperfect subjunctive fosse used in a contrary-to-fact clause). 'Il gran prete' = 'the great/high priest' — the cause that prevented his full reform.
segments.inferno-27-g75: A cui mal prenda! is an expletive curse: a cui = 'to whom', mal prenda = subjunctive 'may misfortune/bad take him' (prenda = prenda, subjunctive of prendere). It's a short wish/cursing formula aimed at the priest.
segments.inferno-27-g76: This segment is a single punctuation mark (comma) continuing the sentence; it links the curse/exclamation to the following relative clause.
segments.inferno-27-g77: Che mi rimise ne le prime colpe: che = 'who/which' introducing a relative clause; mi = 'me' (object); rimise is passato remoto of rimettere or rimisare with sense 'caused to relapse/put back' (here 'put me back'); ne le prime colpe = 'into the first sins' (ne = in/into).
segments.inferno-27-g78: E come e quare: 'and how and why' — a two-part interrogative phrase (quare = why). The repetition e ... e links the two interrogatives in parallel.
segments.inferno-27-g79: Voglio che m’intenda: voglio = 'I want'; che introduces a subordinate clause with intenda (present subjunctive of intendere) meaning 'that he make me understand' or 'that I be made to understand'; m’ = 'me'. The subjunctive marks the speaker's desire/request.
segments.inferno-27-g80: Mentre ch’io forma fui ... che la madre mi diè: 'Mentre' = 'while/when'. Ch’ (che) introduces a subordinate temporal clause. 'Io forma fui' is poetic inversion: fui forma = 'I was formed'; forma here is a noun 'form/shape' (formed into a body). 'D’ossa e di polpe' = 'of bones and of flesh'. 'Che la madre mi diè' is a relative clause 'which my mother gave me' with diè (passato remoto of dare).
segments.inferno-27-g81: L’opere mie non furon leonine: l'opere mie = 'my works/deeds' (opere used as plural noun); non furon = passato remoto 'were not'; leonine = 'lion-like' (adjective). The clause states the character of his actions.
segments.inferno-27-g82: Ma di volpe: 'but of fox' i.e. 'but craftily like a fox' — a short antithetical clause describing the nature of his deeds. Volpe can mean literally 'fox' or figuratively 'a cunning person'.
segments.inferno-27-g128: This segment contains only the punctuation mark (a period) marking the end of the preceding sentence; there are no lexical items to parse or inflect.
segments.inferno-27-g129: "A Minòs mi portò;" is a simple past (passato remoto) clause: A Minòs = 'to Minos' (prepositional phrase of destination), mi = direct object 'me', and portò = 3rd person singular passato remoto of portare meaning 'he brought/took'. The subject is omitted and supplied from context; the semicolon separates this clause from what follows.
segments.inferno-27-g130: The conjunction e links to the next action. quelli = 'that one / he' (refers back to the judge figure) is the subject; attorse is the passato remoto (3rd sg.) of an older verb meaning 'twisted/wound'; otto volte is an adverbial expression 'eight times' placed after the verb; la coda = 'the tail' is the direct object; al dosso duro = 'to/around the hard back' uses the contracted preposition al + noun to indicate the surface around which the tail is wound.
segments.inferno-27-g131: This clause begins with e poi che = 'and then/after that' introducing a temporal/causal detail. per gran rabbia = 'through/because of great rage' (per indicating cause), la = direct object 'it' (referring back to la coda), si = reflexive/clitic marking the action as occurring upon itself or marking the subject's involvement, and morse = passato remoto of mordere, 'he bit'.
segments.inferno-27-g132: disse is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of dire acting as a narrative verb introducing direct speech: 'he said:'. It stands alone here to lead into the quotation.
segments.inferno-27-g133: This is the reported utterance. Questi = 'this one/he' (demonstrative subject of the reported clause), è = present indicative of essere 'is', d’ i rei del foco = 'of the guilty/sinners of the fire' (di + definite article), and furo is an archaic/poetic past plural form (roughly equivalent to furono) used in the line to complete the meter — here part of the older inflected phrase. The whole phrase identifies the person as one of the sinners punished by fire.
segments.inferno-27-g134: per ch’io = 'wherefore/therefore that I' (perché/for which reason), là dove vedi = 'there where you see (me)' (dove introduces the relative place clause; vedi is 2nd singular present of vedere), son = archaic/poetic contraction for sono 'I am', and perduto = past participle used adjectivally 'lost' (so: 'I am lost'). The clause gives the consequence/reason introduced by per.
segments.inferno-27-g135: e sì vestito, = 'and so clothed/dressed,' with sì as an adverb 'thus/so' and vestito a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'dressed' or 'clothed'; it modifies the speaker's current condition and links to the following gerund.
segments.inferno-27-g136: andando is the gerund of andare 'going' and functions adverbially to indicate simultaneous action or circumstance ('while/as I am going' or 'walking'); it connects the state described (so clothed) to the speaker's ongoing motion.
segments.inferno-27-g137: mi = clitic first-person object 'me' and rancuro is the 1st person singular present (or present-in-sense) of the verb rancurare 'to grieve/complain/feel resentment'; together mi rancuro expresses the speaker's suffering or lamentation ('I grieve' / 'I lament myself').
segments.inferno-27-g138: Quand’ elli ebbe ’l suo dir così compiuto = 'When he had thus completed his speech'. Quand’ (quando) is 'when'; ebbe is passato remoto of avere used here as a perfect auxiliary (literally 'had'); 'il suo dir' = 'his word/speech' (dir as a substantive from dire); così compiuto = 'thus completed', with compiuto a past participle describing the completed action.
segments.inferno-27-g139: la fiamma dolorando si partio = 'the flame, grieving, departed.' la fiamma is the subject; dolorando is a gerund/participial form indicating manner ('grieving' or 'in sorrow'); si partio is passato remoto (partì) with the reflexive clitic si, so 'departed' or 'moved off'.
segments.inferno-27-g140: torcendo e dibattendo ’l corno aguto = two gerundives/participles describing simultaneous motion: torcendo = 'twisting', e dibattendo = 'and thrashing/striking about', followed by '’l corno aguto' = 'the sharp/pointed horn' (corno = horn; aguto = 'sharp/pointed' as an adjective). The participles indicate how the flame moved as it left.
segments.inferno-27-g141: Noi passamm’ oltre = 'We passed on' or 'We went on.' Noi = 'we' (subject); passamm’ is a clipped form of passammo (passato remoto, 1st pers. pl.) 'we passed'; oltre = 'beyond/onward'. The clause is a simple narrative motion statement.
segments.inferno-27-g142: e io e ’l duca mio = 'and I and my leader/guide.' This is a coordinate subject: e ... e = 'both ... and'; io = 'I'; 'l duca mio' = 'my duke/leader' (duca used here as 'guide' rather than a noble title).
segments.inferno-27-g143: This longer prepositional/relative construction describes the route and destination: su per lo scoglio = 'up along the rock/cliff' (su + per indicating upward motion along), infino in su l’altr’ arco = 'as far as up to the other arch' (infino in su = 'even/up to'), che cuopre ’l fosso = 'that covers/spans the trench' (che introduces the relative clause, cuopre = present indicative 'covers/spans'), in che si paga il fio = 'in which the penalty/price is paid' (si paga is the impersonal/passive construction 'is paid'), a quei che scommettendo acquistan carco = 'to those who, by betting, acquire/bring upon themselves a load/burden' (scommettendo gerund 'by betting', acquistan = 'they acquire', carco = 'burden/load'). The whole segment combines place, relative clause, and the purpose/result describing who is punished there.

sentences.inferno-27-s5: I was still down below, attentive and bowed, when my guide touched me on the side, saying, "You speak; this one is Latin."
sentences.inferno-27-s6: And I, who already had the answer ready, began to speak without delay: "O soul that art hidden down there, Romagna is not yours, nor ever was — without war in the hearts of its tyrants; but in the open none did I leave there now."
sentences.inferno-27-s7: Ravenna remains as it has been for many years: the eagle of the Polenta family broods over it there, so that Cervia covers it with its vanni.
sentences.inferno-27-s8: The land that has already undergone the long trial and (that) made a bloody host of Franceschi lies beneath the green branches.
sentences.inferno-27-s25: Francis then came, as I lay dead, on my account; but one of the black cherubim said to them: "Do not carry (him): do me no wrong."
sentences.inferno-27-s26: He must be brought down among my wretched ones, for he gave the fraudulent counsel; from that counsel onward they have been in this state down to the hairs; one cannot absolve whoever does not repent, nor can repenting and willing exist together because of the contradiction that will not allow it.
sentences.inferno-27-s28: how I started back when he seized me, saying to me: "Perhaps you did not think that I was a logician!"
sentences.inferno-27-s9: And the old mastiff and the young one of Verrucchio, who made of Montagna a bad government, there where they are wont to make a sucking with their teeth.
sentences.inferno-27-s10: The towns on the Lamone and the Santerno are led by the little lion from the white nest, which changes its place from summer to winter.
sentences.inferno-27-s11: And that one which the Savio wets on its flank, just as it lies between the plain and the hill, lives amid tyranny and yet is a free state.
sentences.inferno-27-s12: Now who are you? I beg you to tell us about it; do not be more harsh than others have been, if your name in the world holds its front (if your reputation stands).
sentences.inferno-27-s1: The flame was already upright and quiet — not to say more — and already, with the sweet poet's permission, it had left us, when another, following behind it, made us turn our eyes to its summit because of a confused sound that issued from it.
sentences.inferno-27-s2: As the Sicilian ox that bellowed earlier at that man's crying — and rightly so, for he had tuned it with his file — bellowed with the voice of the afflicted, so that although it was of copper it nevertheless seemed pierced through by grief; likewise, because there was no passage or opening from the start in the fire, the bitter words were changed into its language.
sentences.inferno-27-s3: But after they had raised their passage up to the point, giving it that little jerk which the tongue had given during their passage, we heard say: “O you to whom I direct my voice, and you who were just now speaking Lombard, saying ‘Istra ten va, I no longer urge you’, because I may perhaps have arrived somewhat late, do not be unwilling to remain and speak with me; see that it does not trouble me — and I burn!”
sentences.inferno-27-s4: If you have indeed now fallen into this blind world from that sweet Latin land from which I bring all my guilt, tell me whether the Romagnoli have peace or war; for I was from the mountains there within Urbino and the ridge from which the Tiber parts.
sentences.inferno-27-s21: But as Constantine asked Sylvester, from within the Siratti, to cure leprosy, so this man asked me to be his teacher to cure his proud fever, asking me for counsel — and I was silent because his words seemed drunken.
sentences.inferno-27-s22: Then he repeated: "Let not your heart suspect; up to now I absolve you, and you teach me to do so as Penestrino throws into the earth."
sentences.inferno-27-s23: I can shut and unshut heaven, as you know; therefore there are two keys that my predecessor did not hold dear.
sentences.inferno-27-s24: Then the grave arguments pressed upon me where silence had warned me of the worst, and I said: "Father, since you wash me of that sin into which I must now fall, a long promise together with brief waiting will make you triumph in the high seat."
sentences.inferno-27-s17: I knew all the stratagems and the hidden ways, and so I put their art into practice, so that the sound went out to the ends of the earth.
sentences.inferno-27-s18: When I found myself arrived at that part of my life where everyone ought to lower the sails and furl the rigging, what formerly pleased me then displeased me, and I made myself penitent and confessed; ah, miserable, weary!
sentences.inferno-27-s19: and it would have done me good.
sentences.inferno-27-s20: The prince of the new Pharisees, having war near the Lateran — and not with Saracens nor with Jews, for every one of his enemies was a Christian — and no one had been to take Acre nor been a merchant in the Sultan's land, nor did he regard in himself the highest office nor sacred orders, nor (did he see) in me that halter which used to make his own belts leaner.
sentences.inferno-27-s13: After the fire had roared a little in its own fashion, it moved its sharp point this way, that way, and then gave such a blast: "If I believed that my answer were to someone who would ever return to the world, this flame would remain without further agitation; but since never yet has anyone returned alive from this depth — if I hear the truth — I answer you without fear of disgrace."
sentences.inferno-27-s14: I was a man-at-arms, and then I was a corded friar, thinking myself, thus girt, to make amends; and certainly my belief would have been whole, if it were not for the high priest — may ill befall him!
sentences.inferno-27-s15: — who put me back into my first sins; and how and why, I want him to make (me) understand.
sentences.inferno-27-s16: While I was formed of bones and flesh that my mother gave me, my deeds were not lion-like, but of the fox.
sentences.inferno-27-s30: He brought me to Minos; and he wound his tail eight times about his hard back; and then, in great rage, he bit it, and said: "This one is of the guilty of the fire"; therefore I am lost there where you see me, and so clothed, walking, I lament.
sentences.inferno-27-s31: When he had thus completed his speech, the flame, grieving, departed, twisting and thrashing its sharp horn.
sentences.inferno-27-s32: We went on, both I and my guide, up along the rock as far as the other arch that spans the trench where the penalty is paid by those who, by betting, earn a burden.
